1 
  
COMMENT  
March 20 , 2025     Via Docket Upload   
 
The Honorable Sean Duffy  
U.S. Secretary of Transportation  
1200 New Jersey Ave SE,  
Washington, DC 20590  
 
Re: SBTC’s SUPPLEMENTAL  COMMENTS ON TRANSPARENCY IN PROPERTY 
BROKER TRANSACTIONS (FMCSA -2023 -0257)  
 
Dear Secretary Duffy : 
 
Comes now, the Small Business in Transportation Coalition, Inc. (“SBTC”) offering our 
supplemental comments on the above -referenced docket.  
By way of background, the SBTC is a 21,000+ member 501(c)(6) non -profit 
transportation industry trade group, which promotes and protects the interests of small 
businesses in the industry.  We encourage ethical business practices and support 
teamwork, cooperation, transparency, and partnerships among our trucker, small carrier, 
small broker, forwarder & shipper  members  who seek to network and do business fairly 
and with the utmost integrity. SBTC therefore represents various stakeholders in th is 
transparency discussion , which we believe , amounts not so much to carrier versus 
broker, but big business versus small  business .  
The record reflects that FMCSA opened comments in this matter  on November 20, 
2024. The comment period closed on January 21, 202 5. SBTC filed its comments 
directly to the President on January 21, 2025 , as there was no Secretary yet under the 
new Administration , which had assumed office a mere 24 hours prior.   
1775 I. Street NW, Ste 1150, Washington, DC 20006 (202) 587 -2751  
2 
 The Honorable Secretary Sean Duffy  
March 20, 2025  
Immediately before the comment period closed, SBTC petitioned FMCSA to extend  the 
comment period due to an influx of comments still coming in. FMCSA advised we could 
petition to reopen the comment period, which we immediately did thereafter. FMCSA 
then reopened the docket  for 30 days on February 18, 2025 and comments  close  today.  
Disturbingly, SBTC Board Member Dakota Springfields of Pink C heetah Express 
received a response from the FMCSA FOIA Unit to her 2024 FOIA request immediately 
after  the first comment period closed , in which FMCSA releas ed to her a never -before -
seen November 30, 202 3 directive from Nelson Newcomb of  FMCSA to Total Quality 
Logistics (TQ L) ordering the broker to (1) remove the  waiver of 371.3 language from 
their broker -carrier contract citing the Evasion of Regulation statute (49 USC §14906 ); 
and (2) to comply with 371.3 records requests  from carriers moving forward , which is 
exactly what Springfi elds asked  the Secretary to do in her October 31, 2023 complaint .  
Ms. Springfields ’ company is now suing TQL1 as imme diately after FMCSA issued the 
November 30, 2023 directive, TQL allegedly ignored her December 202 3 request for 
records on 14 additional loads and blocked her from communicating  further with TQL.  
SBTC appreciates  FMCSA gave stakeholders additional time to comment. It appears 
from the docket the industry took advantage of this opportunity to chime in, especially 
after th is important November 30, 2023 directive to TQL become public.  
The SBTC now supplements its original comments on the basis of the November 30, 
2023 directive  and offers the following ... SBTC contends that the FMCSA ’s initial 
decision in the  November 2024 proposed rulemaking announcement not to approve  the 
requests of petitioners SBTC and OOIDA to prohib it waivers is disingenuous , 
misguided, arbitrary and capricious. FMCSA cannot , on the one hand , in response to a 
complaint , direct the s econd biggest broker in America to remove 49 CFR 371.3 waivers 
from its  broker -carrier contracts in what FMCSA apparently believe d was private 
correspondence between the agency and the broker that was not initially  made 
available to the compl ainant , and then refrain from directing  the entire brokerage 
industry  to remove such waivers on the other, without engaging in disc riminatory, 
arbitrary , and cap ricious  activity in violation of the Administra tive Procedure Act.  This 
inconsistency  will not be tolerated with impunity , and will not withstand judicial scrutiny.  
Furthermore, SBTC mad e an expedited FOIA request with FMCSA over the past month 
asking for Newcomb ’s report of his investigation of the Pink Cheetah Express Complaint 
against TQL to be used in this comment. After initially violating FOIA  with no response 
on the petition for expedited handling , FMCSA  then skirted the request  outright , 
suggesting there was never formally a n “investigation ” opened  by Newcomb .  
 
1 1:25 -cv-00552 -SLS PINK CHEETAH EXPRESS, LLC v. TOTAL QUALITY LOGISTICS, LLC Sparkle L. Sooknanan, presiding 
Date filed: 02/25/2025  3 
 The Honorable Secretary Sean Duffy  
March 20, 2025  
SBTC then replied that it was requesting a copy of the report of Newcomb ’s “review .” It 
has not been provided by FMCSA in time to file this report. We would concede  this 
matter of semanti cs coul d just be  a misunderstanding , but not for the fact that FMCSA 
still owes SBTC ’s undersigned executive director a complete response to a FOIA 
request filed in November 2016, which is the agency ’s oldest open FOIA request. The 
SBTC therefore contends FMCSA is  now willing ly obstructing the SBTC ’s receipt of 
information  in this matter  as well .        
The S BTC once again implores you as Secretary to direct FMCSA to prohibit 
contractual waivers to avoid litigation over the final r ule in the post -Chevron era and 
follow the precedent New comb set in the November 30, 2023 directive to TQL and 
prohibit waivers as unlawful evasion of regulation  in a non -discriminatory, non -
preferential fashion as a matter of equ al protection of the law under the Fifth 
Amendment . 
Following OOIDA ’s suggestion, t he SBTC also implores you to direct the agency to 
make the 48 hour  electronic release of rate transparency data automatic to prevent 
blacklisting  of carriers who are otherwise left to have to file a request for same to 
prevent brokers  from having the opportunity to engage in unreasonable restraint of 
trade in violation of the Sherman Antitrust Act.   
Lastly, we again note FMCSA has  removed language from the original rule that perhaps 
the agency  feels is included generally in the proposed rule dealing with itemization. We 
recommend you direct FMCSA to put the following language back in so there is no 
wiggle room for broker to not provide this information. This rule is supposed to be about 
strengthening transparency, not deregulating it.  
(4) The amount of compensation received by the broker for the brokerage service 
performed and the name of the payer;  
(6) The amount of any freight charges collected by the broker and the date of 
payment to the carrier.  
 
Thank you.  
Sincerely,  
/s/ JAMES LAMB  
SBTC Executive Director  
 
cc:   laurence.socci@soccilawfirm.com
